normalization.notes: Modern Hangul syllable blocks are stored as precomposed characters (U+AC00–U+D7A3). NFC normalizes to precomposed form. Jamo-decomposed forms (NFD) are used only for internal analysis, never for learner-visible content.

segmentation.notes: Korean uses whitespace word boundaries, unlike Thai and Chinese. UAX#29 word break is sufficient for coarse segmentation. For jamo-level grapheme analysis, decompose syllable blocks using the Unicode Hangul syllable decomposition formula: codepoint - 0xAC00, then split into choseong/jungseong/jongseong indices.

sources.notes: National Institute of Korean Language (NIKL) balanced corpus. Large, authoritative, genre-balanced. License requires institutional review before use beyond personal research — keep discovery_only until confirmed.

SUBTLEX-KO subtitle-based word frequency list. CC BY 4.0 confirmed for the frequency list itself. Good proxy for everyday spoken and informal Korean. Cleared for scoring; review before any shipped content cites it.

Korean Wiktionary entries provide glosses, part of speech, and basic usage. CC BY-SA 3.0 confirmed. Share-alike applies to derivative works — keep scoring_only until legal review confirms shipped gloss strings are acceptable derivatives.

Korean tokenizer and NLP tool library, not a text corpus. Apache 2.0. Used as an analysis tool for segmentation experiments, not as a content source. domain_weight 0 because it contributes no scored content directly.

Namu Wiki — large Korean community encyclopedia. CC BY-SA 2.0 KR confirmed. Good for culture and media domain coverage. Share-alike applies; keep scoring_only until legal review confirms shipped excerpts are acceptable.
